evidence: goseg/system/wifi_transport_adapter.go combines websocket transport lifecycle (upgrade/read loop/client tracking), domain command handling (`payload.Payload.Action == "connect"`), and host process control (`systemctl restart groundseg`) in one adapter type.

goseg/system/wifi_transport_adapter.go hard-codes action routing with a string literal (`"connect"`) while goseg/uploadsvc/service.go models actions as a typed command surface (`type Action`, `Command`, `Executor`), showing inconsistent top-level orchestration patterns across neighboring subsystems.
goseg/uploadsvc/service.go maintains both a global `supportedActions` list and an executor-local dispatch map, creating split ownership for action capability declaration.

goseg/startram/errors.go is a very thin policy wrapper file (single constant and one wrapping function), indicating fragmentation of architecture concerns into micro-files without a broader cohesive boundary in this batch.

Runtime seam structs are very wide function bags that mostly mirror package-level functions (`dockerRuntime` in `goseg/docker/runtime_seams.go`, `routineRuntime` in `goseg/routines/runtime_seams.go`) rather than expressing cohesive domain interfaces.

Core configuration is a mutable process-wide singleton with many exported globals and init-time side effects (`globalConfig`, `BasePath`, `Architecture`, `DockerDir`, `GSContainers` in `goseg/config/config.go`), forcing broad implicit dependencies.

Tests repeatedly mutate package globals (`config.DockerDir` in `goseg/docker/wireguard_test.go`, `config.Architecture` in `goseg/routines/version_test.go`, `leak.LeakChan` in `goseg/rectify/rectify_test.go`), indicating seams are coupled to global state instead of explicit dependencies.

Integration between event producers and UI state is stringly typed and fragmented: transition/event types are hard-coded strings in multiple modules (`goseg/rectify/rectify.go`, `goseg/routines/docker.go`, `goseg/handler/startram.go`).

Boundary mapping is not isolated: urbit view assembly performs live config loads and runtime fetches inside mapping (`assemble` in `goseg/broadcast/collectors_and_mappers.go`) while orchestration is elsewhere (`ConstructPierInfo` in `goseg/broadcast/broadcast.go`).
Cross-module orchestration is duplicated in handlers and routines (container status changes, broadcast updates, config toggles), increasing seam entropy and drift risk.

Large repetitive switch blocks update broadcast state field-by-field (`UrbitTransitionHandler`, `NewShipTransitionHandler`, `RectifyUrbit` in `goseg/rectify/rectify.go`), making control flow verbose and fragile when adding transition types.

Long-running loops have no cancellation path and rely on forever receives/ticks (`DockerListener` and `dockerSubscriptionHandler` in `goseg/routines/docker.go`, `wifiInfoLoop` in `goseg/system/wifi.go`), reducing lifecycle clarity.
Repetitive update-then-broadcast boilerplate appears in many branches instead of a single helper path (`goseg/rectify/rectify.go`, `goseg/routines/docker.go`).

Urbit lifecycle stop/wait/persist logic is duplicated across modules (`WaitComplete` and `persistShipConf` in both `goseg/chopsvc/service.go` and `goseg/handler/urbit.go`), indicating unresolved structural reuse.

Handler files combine transport dispatch with long-running orchestration and infrastructure control (for example `SupportHandler` in `goseg/handler/support.go`, `StartramHandler` + `handleStartramToggle` in `goseg/handler/startram.go`, and ship provisioning flow in `goseg/handler/newship.go`).

`goseg/config/config.go` acts as a monolith for startup bootstrap, persistence, runtime state cache, settings mutation API, and utility concerns, while `goseg/config/urbit.go` further couples config persistence with Docker inspection.

findings.summary: The Wi‑Fi transport adapter owns unrelated layers (websocket transport, command semantics, and process restart policy), blurring subsystem ownership boundaries.
evidence: captiveTransportAdapter manages websocket upgrader/client registry/read loop and also performs command interpretation in `processMessage`.
`processMessage` triggers infrastructure side effects via `connectToWiFi` and `restartGroundSeg`, while `restartGroundSegService` shells out to `systemctl restart groundseg`.
In contrast, uploadsvc/service.go separates command dispatch behind `Executor` and `Service`, highlighting that system package bypasses a comparable application-service seam.
findings.suggestion: Extract a captive portal application service that receives typed commands and owns Wi‑Fi/restart orchestration, while keeping `captiveTransportAdapter` limited to transport concerns (decode/encode, connection lifecycle). Route `processMessage` through that service instead of performing side effects directly.

findings.summary: Action contract definition is inconsistent and partially duplicated, reducing predictability of top-level behavior ownership.
evidence: wifi transport uses raw string matching (`payload.Payload.Action != "connect"`) with no typed contract.
uploadsvc defines typed actions (`ActionOpenEndpoint`, `ActionReset`) plus `UnsupportedActionError`, but also keeps a separate global `supportedActions` slice and dispatch map in `Executor`.
adapter test asserts mapping by converting action constants to/from strings, showing cross-layer dependence on literal protocol values instead of a single canonical action contract owner.
findings.suggestion: Standardize action handling on a single contract pattern: define typed action enums plus parse/validate helpers at boundary adapters, and make one source of truth for supported actions (derive from dispatch or registration API, not parallel lists).

findings.summary: Global singleton state plus wide function-bag runtimes creates high indirection without clear domain boundaries.
evidence: `config` exposes mutable globals and does heavy init-time setup in `init()`; many packages read/write through this shared state.
`dockerRuntime` and `routineRuntime` each carry dozens of function fields and mostly re-export package calls, indicating indirection wrappers instead of cohesive service contracts.
Entry points wire subsystems through globals rather than explicit lifecycle-scoped dependencies.
findings.suggestion: Introduce cohesive service interfaces (e.g., ConfigStore, ContainerRuntime, TransitionPublisher) with constructor injection; reduce runtime structs to capability-focused dependencies and phase out direct use of mutable config globals.

findings.summary: Tests depend on mutating package globals, showing production seams are not explicit or isolated.
evidence: `TestWriteWgConfCreateAndUpdate` mutates `config.DockerDir` globally.
Version tests mutate global `config.Architecture`.
Rectify tests replace global channel `leak.LeakChan` to keep handlers unblocked.
findings.suggestion: Move mutable process config and channels behind injectable test fixtures/interfaces so tests can provide local instances without cross-test global rewiring.

findings.summary: Cross-module handoffs use ad-hoc string event types, making integration contracts brittle and hard to evolve safely.
evidence: Transition handlers branch on many raw string constants (`pack`, `toggle`, `register`, etc.) with no compile-time linkage across producers and consumers.
Different modules publish and interpret overlapping event strings independently.
Unknown-type handling is runtime-only logging, so drift is detected late.
findings.suggestion: Define typed transition/event enums and shared payload structs in one contract package; centralize publish/apply helpers so producers and consumers share a single protocol surface.

findings.summary: Broadcast assembly mixes mapping with live IO and config mutation reads, obscuring boundary responsibilities and creating N+1 seam behavior.
evidence: `urbitViewMapper.assemble` calls `config.LoadUrbitConfig`, `config.UrbitConf`, `docker.GetContainerStats`, and other runtime lookups while also mapping output fields.
`ConstructPierInfo` already prepares runtime snapshots, but mapper still performs additional per-pier lookups and side-dependent reads.
Data translation and data acquisition are not in one obvious seam, making behavior harder to reason about and test.
findings.suggestion: Split into explicit phases: collect all runtime/config inputs once, then run pure mapping over immutable snapshots; keep IO in collectors and keep mappers deterministic.

findings.summary: Transition handlers repeat state-fetch/mutate/publish blocks extensively, increasing branch complexity and maintenance risk.
evidence: Many switch cases in `rectify.go` duplicate `current := broadcast.GetState()`, mutate one field, then `UpdateBroadcast` and `BroadcastToClients`.
Similar state-change + broadcast patterns appear in docker routine handlers and maintenance flows.
The repeated imperative pattern increases chances of inconsistent behavior across event types.
findings.suggestion: Introduce small helpers for transition mutation/publish (or table-driven dispatch) so each case only declares field mapping and optional side effects.

findings.summary: Config rendering is a monolithic raw string block that obscures intent and invites formatting drift.
evidence: makeConfig (lines 31-83) validates inputs, then emits a large multi-line literal with embedded placeholders and inconsistent indentation.
The generated content mixes concerns (validation assumptions, defaults, protocol comments, and serialization) in one expression, so a tiny change requires editing an 80+ line literal.
findings.suggestion: Split rendering into small named sections/constants (base radio settings, auth settings, optional legacy section) and assemble with a strings.Builder or text/template; keep validation separate from serialization so each step is easier to reason about and test.

findings.summary: writeHostapdConfig relies on implicit package globals, reducing local clarity and testability.
evidence: writeHostapdConfig (line 18) calls makeConfig(wlan, ssid, password) without receiving those values as parameters.
The function’s behavior and output depend on external mutable state rather than explicit inputs, which makes control flow harder to reason about in isolation.
findings.suggestion: Change writeHostapdConfig to accept wlan/ssid/password (or a small typed config struct) as arguments and pass them through explicitly; keep only file I/O inside the writer function.

findings.summary: The handler package mixes unrelated capabilities (auth/session, ship lifecycle, connectivity, dev operations), obscuring ownership and change boundaries.
evidence: goseg/handler/handler.go includes LoginHandler/LogoutHandler, NewShipHandler, C2CHandler, UnauthHandler, and PwHandler in one file.
goseg/handler/dev.go adds development backup/restore and StarTram reminder controls in the same package, with direct imports of backupsvc, startram, click, config, and system.
goseg/handler/dev_test.go must reset many package-level seams for these mixed responsibilities, signaling weak internal boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Split handler into bounded subpackages/files by capability (e.g., handler/auth, handler/ship, handler/connectivity, handler/devtools) with a thin transport entry layer that dispatches to dedicated services.

findings.summary: The click package acts as a broad multi-domain facade, flattening distinct domains into one API surface and weakening package role clarity.
evidence: goseg/click/click.go exports unrelated concerns together: FixAcme, desk lifecycle methods, SendNotification, storage link/unlink, RestoreTlon, BackupTlon.
goseg/click/code.go keeps +code cache state and click execution plumbing in the same package API family rather than behind narrower domain modules.
goseg/click/click_test.go validates dispatch via package-level seam overrides, reflecting a large, centralized package surface.
findings.suggestion: Refactor click into explicit domain subpackages (e.g., click/desk, click/notify, click/backup, click/luscode) and keep click root as a minimal composition layer or remove it entirely.

findings.summary: Background routines combine scheduler lifecycle, policy decisions, and direct infrastructure calls, making directory boundaries in routines ambiguous.
evidence: goseg/routines/backup.go contains infinite-loop scheduling, backup window policy (time checks), and direct backupsvc/config calls in one unit.
goseg/routines/chop.go contains periodic scheduling plus direct docker stats access and chop execution policy.
Both files define package-level seam vars and mutable globals, suggesting routines owns both orchestration and service logic instead of delegating policy/services behind clearer boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Separate routines into scheduler drivers (tick/lifecycle) and injected policy/service components (backup policy, chop policy, infrastructure adapters) so routines directory represents orchestration only.

findings.summary: Upload action compatibility is governed by duplicated sources of truth instead of one contract surface.
evidence: README.md requires dispatch-table parity for every value in `SupportedActions()`.
goseg/uploadsvc/service.go defines `supportedActions` separately from the executor `dispatch` map, so action support is maintained in two independent structures.
Executor.SupportedActions() filters by `dispatch` presence, which can hide drift between declared and actually routable actions instead of failing fast.
The reviewed adapter test covers only `open-endpoint` mapping and does not enforce whole-action contract completeness.
findings.suggestion: Refactor to a single action registry (action -> handler metadata) that drives both `SupportedActions()` and dispatch, and add a deterministic table-driven contract test that fails when any declared action lacks routing.

findings.summary: Command-routing contracts are modeled inconsistently across subsystems, reducing architectural predictability.
evidence: goseg/system/wifi_transport_adapter.go routes websocket messages by checking `payload.Payload.Action != "connect"` with a raw literal.
goseg/uploadsvc/service.go formalizes actions via `type Action string` constants and explicit unsupported-action errors.
README.md positions backend boundaries as contract-driven, but these two runtime paths apply different contract styles for similar message-dispatch behavior.
findings.suggestion: Adopt a shared command-contract pattern for websocket action handling (typed action enums + explicit unsupported-action behavior) in system transport paths to match service-layer contract rigor.

findings.summary: Governance policy is concentrated in README prose instead of being co-located with enforceable contract artifacts near runtime boundaries.
evidence: README.md contains detailed boundary and quality-gate obligations (masked external errors and upload dispatch parity).

goseg/startram/errors.go and goseg/uploadsvc/service.go implement relevant contract surfaces but do not embed adjacent self-checking mechanisms (e.g., package-local invariant assertions or contract-oriented tests in the reviewed file set).
This split makes architectural guarantees depend on external process discipline rather than discoverable ownership at the boundary modules themselves.
findings.suggestion: Move key governance rules into code-adjacent contract artifacts: package docs plus boundary-focused tests that codify the guarantees where the contracts live, leaving README as summary rather than primary policy source.

findings.summary: Urbit lifecycle helpers are duplicated across packages instead of being owned by one domain service.
evidence: `WaitComplete` in both files has near-identical timeout/poll/retry/status-check flow against `docker.GetShipStatus` with the same failure-counter behavior.
Both files define a `persistShipConf` helper with the same `config.UpdateUrbit` mutation closure (`*conf = shipConf`).
The duplicated helpers are used in operational paths (`ChopPier`/delete flows), so behavior changes can drift between copies.
findings.suggestion: Move wait/persist lifecycle primitives into a single shared Urbit lifecycle component (for example under `chopsvc` or a dedicated `shipworkflow` service) and have handlers call that component instead of carrying their own copies.

findings.summary: Transition/event lifecycle handling is scattered and inconsistently encoded across handlers.
evidence: `SupportHandler` manually emits loading/error/reset transitions with hardcoded deferred sleeps and reset events.
`handleStartramRestart`/`handleStartramToggle` partially use `publishTransitionWithPolicy`, but still embed event-type-specific reset payload forms (`nil`, empty string, or explicit data) inline.
`newship.go` publishes transitions directly (`bootStage`, `error`, `patp`) without a common lifecycle wrapper, unlike other handler flows.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single transition orchestration abstraction (state machine or typed helper) for start/success/error/reset semantics, and migrate handler actions to declarative transition steps rather than ad-hoc publish/sleep blocks.

findings.summary: Configuration modules mix unrelated responsibilities (bootstrap, runtime caches, persistence, and external system probing).
evidence: `config/config.go` `init()` performs environment bootstrap, file/key creation, defaults merge, binary hashing, persistence, and starts background channel processing in one package entrypoint.
The same file owns both typed patch API (`ConfPatch`/`With*` options) and legacy raw map mutation (`UpdateConf`), creating two mutation paths to one store.

`config/urbit.go` persistence path (`persistUrbitConfigLocked`) also performs Docker container inspection (`getImageTagByContainerName`) before writing config, crossing config/storage and runtime-infra boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Split config responsibilities into separate components: bootstrapping, repository/persistence, runtime snapshot access, and runtime enrichers (e.g., image tag resolvers). Keep one mutation pathway and make runtime-derived fields explicit outside persistence writes.

findings.summary: Auth package combines token cryptography, HTTP identity checks, websocket client registry, and session persistence in one module.
evidence: `auth/auth.go` includes token generation/encryption (`CreateToken`, `KeyfileEncrypt/Decrypt`, `AuthToken`) while also managing live connection maps (`AddToAuthMap`, `RemoveFromAuthMap`, `Ws*` helpers).
`ValidateAndAuthorizeRequestToken` mixes HTTP request fingerprinting (`X-Forwarded-For`, `User-Agent`) with token mutation and authorization checks.
Login flow in `handler/handler.go` directly depends on this all-in-one module for both auth decision and websocket/session side effects, tightening coupling.
findings.suggestion: Separate auth into at least three collaborators: token service (crypto + claims), request authenticator (context validation), and session/connection registry (websocket state + persistence). Keep handlers orchestrating these explicit interfaces instead of one omnibus package.

findings.summary: Handler layer performs deep domain workflows and infrastructure mutations instead of delegating to cohesive application services.
evidence: `NewShipHandler` validates protocol payloads, derives keys, resolves drives, and dispatches destructive operations; `createUrbitShip` then manipulates Docker volumes/containers, config state, and StarTram registration.
`StartramHandler` switch branches build inline closures and trigger asynchronous orchestration functions that themselves perform cross-module config/docker/startram operations.
`urbit_operations.go` centralizes many unrelated actions (domain aliasing, export, power/network toggles, backups, chop scheduling), creating a broad command surface in one file.
findings.suggestion: Refactor handlers into thin transport adapters that map request -> command object, with command execution moved into focused application services (ship provisioning, startram orchestration, urbit operations), each with bounded dependencies.
